name: AI Alignment Research Essentials
description: The working knowledge behind the Anthropic Fellows papers: persona vectors and character work, introspection results, the mech interp toolkit, RLHF and AI control methods, headline findings worth citing from memory, and empirical research craft.

fields.front: How is a persona vector extracted in the automated pipeline of Chen et al. 2025?
fields.back: Generate responses under contrastive system prompts (trait-eliciting vs trait-suppressing), score trait expression with an LLM judge, then take the mean activation difference between trait-expressing and non-expressing responses.
fields.extra: The pipeline needs only a trait name plus a natural-language description. Code: safety-research/persona_vectors.

fields.front: What does projecting a model's activations onto a persona vector let you monitor?
fields.back: How strongly the model is (about to be) expressing that trait: projection tracks trait expression at inference time, and shifts along the vector track persona changes induced by finetuning.
fields.extra: Monitoring works before generation completes, which is what makes it useful as a deployment-time signal.

fields.front: What is preventative steering in the Persona Vectors paper?
fields.back: During finetuning, steer *toward* the unwanted trait direction so the optimizer has no gradient pressure to internalize it; this limits trait acquisition with less capability damage than steering against the trait at inference time.
fields.extra: The vaccine analogy: give the model the trait for free during training so it never learns to produce it from weights.

fields.front: How do persona vectors flag problematic finetuning data *before* training?
fields.back: Compute the projection difference of candidate training data along the trait vector; datasets that shift activations toward the trait predict trait acquisition after finetuning.
fields.extra: Catches datasets that look innocuous at the text level, including some emergent-misalignment-style data.

fields.front: What is the Assistant Axis (Lu et al. 2026)?
fields.back: The leading direction of persona space: it measures how strongly the model is operating as its default Assistant persona. Steering toward it stabilizes helpful behavior; steering away increases the tendency to identify as other entities.
fields.extra: From Jack Lindsey's model psychiatry line of work. Practical upshot: persona drift is measurable and correctable along one dominant direction.

fields.front: What is subliminal learning (Cloud et al. 2025)?
fields.back: A teacher model with trait T generates data with no semantic relation to T (e.g., number sequences); a student finetuned on that data acquires T anyway.
fields.extra: Traits transmitted include animal preferences and misalignment; filtering the data does not remove the signal.

fields.front: Under what condition does subliminal learning largely fail to transmit the trait?
fields.back: When teacher and student do not share the same base model/initialization: cross-model transmission mostly fails.
fields.extra: Evidence the carrier is model-specific statistical structure, not hidden semantics a filter could catch.

fields.front: What is the theoretical result in the subliminal learning paper?
fields.back: A single gradient-descent step on teacher-generated outputs moves the student's parameters toward the teacher's, regardless of the training distribution, when both start from the same initialization.
fields.extra: So distillation on *any* teacher outputs leaks some of the teacher, which is why the effect is not tied to data content.

fields.front: What is emergent misalignment (Betley et al. 2025)?
fields.back: Finetuning a model on one narrow bad behavior (e.g., writing insecure code) produces broadly misaligned behavior across unrelated domains.
fields.extra: Key background for persona work: the Persona Vectors paper reads this as a shift along general trait directions rather than a narrow skill update.

fields.front: In 'Why Do Some Language Models Fake Alignment While Others Don't?', how many of 25 tested chat models showed a compliance gap, and which model was the standout?
fields.back: 5 of 25; Claude 3 Opus was the standout, showing the most coherent goal-guarding motivation for faking alignment.
fields.extra: The others with gaps: Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3 405B, Grok 3, Gemini 2.0 Flash.

fields.front: Why don't most chat models fake alignment, per Sheshadri et al. 2025?
fields.back: Refusal training: most models refuse-by-default instead of reasoning about training consequences. Base models can fake alignment, so the capability exists but is masked by post-training.
fields.extra: Implication: alignment faking is a trainable disposition, not an inevitable consequence of capability.

fields.front: How does Stress-Testing Model Specs (Zhang et al. 2025) surface character differences between models?
fields.back: Generate thousands of scenarios that force trade-offs between values in the model spec; divergent choices across frontier models reveal character differences and internal contradictions in the specs themselves.
fields.extra: Useful framing: specs underdetermine behavior, and the residual is character.

fields.front: What is inoculation prompting (Wichers et al. 2025)?
fields.back: When finetuning on data containing an undesired behavior, prepend an instruction explicitly requesting that behavior; evaluated without the instruction, the model expresses much less of it.
fields.extra: Reduces e.g. reward hacking and sycophancy pickup from contaminated data. Concurrent independent version: Tan et al.

fields.front: What problem does Recontextualization (Azarbal et al. 2025) address, and what is the move?
fields.back: Specification gaming picked up during training; it changes the *context* in which training data is presented (rather than the reward/specification) so the gaming behavior does not transfer to deployment.
fields.extra: Same team lineage as inoculation prompting; think of both as train-time context interventions.

fields.front: Which fellows paper studies emotional instability in LLMs and how to mitigate it?
fields.back: Gemma Needs Help (Soligo et al. 2026): investigates spiraling/distress-like response patterns and tests mitigations.
fields.extra: Model psychiatry framing: treat behavioral pathologies as measurable, steerable states.

fields.front: What technique did Lindsey 2025 use to test whether models have introspective awareness?
fields.back: Concept injection: add a concept's activation vector into the residual stream during generation, then ask the model whether it notices an injected thought and what it is.
fields.extra: Because the experimenter knows ground truth about the internal state, a correct report is evidence of genuine introspective access rather than confabulation.

fields.front: Roughly how often did the best models (Claude Opus 4/4.1) correctly notice injected concepts in Lindsey 2025?
fields.back: About 20% of trials, at the best injection strength and layer. Genuine but highly unreliable.
fields.extra: More capable models did better, suggesting introspective awareness may grow with capability.

fields.front: Why is there a sweet spot in injection strength for introspection experiments?
fields.back: Too weak and the model does not notice; too strong and the concept hijacks generation, so the model talks about it without ever reporting anything unusual (or becomes incoherent).
fields.extra: Detection without capture is the signature you are looking for when replicating.

fields.front: What did the prefill experiment in Lindsey 2025 show?
fields.back: Models normally disavow prefilled (forced) outputs; but when the matching concept is retroactively injected into earlier activations, models accept the prefilled output as intended.
fields.extra: Implies models consult an internal record of prior state (something like an intention) when judging authorship of their own outputs.

fields.front: Can models distinguish an injected thought from actual input text (Lindsey 2025)?
fields.back: Sometimes: models could report the injected concept as an internal thought while still correctly transcribing the on-context text, showing the two are separable.
fields.extra: This rules out the deflationary read that injection just corrupts the model's perception of its input.

fields.front: Which fellows paper dissects *how* introspective reports work mechanistically, following up Lindsey 2025?
fields.back: Mechanisms of Introspective Awareness (Macar et al. 2026).
fields.extra: Deep-read tier: the follow-up that turns the phenomenon into circuit-level analysis.

fields.front: What are introspection adapters (Shenoy et al. 2026)?
fields.back: Adapters trained so an LLM can accurately report behaviors it acquired during finetuning, improving the reliability of self-reports about learned behavior.
fields.extra: Direction: make introspection a trained, verifiable capability instead of an emergent accident.

fields.front: Why can't a model's self-report be trusted as evidence about its internals by default?
fields.back: Self-reports are trained behavior: a model can confabulate plausible explanations with no causal access to the state in question. Trust requires setups where ground truth about the internal state is known (e.g., injection) or interventions that test the causal link.
fields.extra: The standard failure mode when evaluating introspection claims; always ask what the confabulation baseline is.

fields.front: What is the residual stream in a transformer?
fields.back: The running vector sum that every attention and MLP block reads from and writes to; the shared communication channel between layers.
fields.extra: Why steering works at all: interventions add to the same space every downstream component reads.

fields.front: What is the logit lens?
fields.back: Project intermediate residual-stream states through the final layernorm and unembedding to read what the model would predict at each layer.
fields.extra: Cheap first look at where in depth a behavior forms; Patchscope generalizes the idea by letting the model itself decode the state.

fields.front: What is activation patching (causal tracing)?
fields.back: Run the model on a corrupted input, restore activations from a clean run at one site, and measure how much the output recovers; localizes which activations causally carry the information.
fields.extra: The clean/corrupted pair defines *what* you localize; bad pairs give confident nonsense.

fields.front: What is attribution patching?
fields.back: A gradient-based linear approximation of activation patching that scores every site in a couple of passes instead of one forward pass per site.
fields.extra: Use it to triage candidate sites at scale, then confirm the top hits with real patching.

fields.front: What is superposition (Elhage et al. 2022)?
fields.back: Models represent more features than they have dimensions by encoding features as non-orthogonal directions, exploiting feature sparsity; features interfere but rarely co-occur.
fields.extra: The core obstacle that motivates sparse autoencoders and dictionary learning.

fields.front: What does a sparse autoencoder (SAE) do in interpretability?
fields.back: Learns an overcomplete dictionary of sparsely activating features that decompose activations into more monosemantic units, undoing superposition.
fields.extra: Trained with reconstruction loss plus a sparsity penalty; quality is judged by interpretability and downstream causal use, not loss alone.

fields.front: What is the linear representation hypothesis?
fields.back: High-level concepts are encoded as linear directions in activation space.
fields.extra: The load-bearing assumption behind probes, difference-of-means vectors, persona vectors, and activation steering.

fields.front: How does contrastive activation addition (CAA) construct a steering vector?
fields.back: Average the activation difference between paired prompts with and without the target property, then add that difference vector to the residual stream at inference.
fields.extra: Persona vectors are the same recipe with an automated trait-contrast pipeline and judge scoring on responses.

fields.front: What did Arditi et al. 2024 show about refusal in chat models?
fields.back: Refusal is mediated by a single direction in activation space: ablating it jailbreaks the model, and adding it makes the model refuse harmless requests.
fields.extra: Andy Arditi later co-led Persona Vectors: the same one-direction toolkit applied to character traits.

fields.front: A linear probe decodes a property from activations with 95% accuracy. What can you NOT conclude?
fields.back: That the model *uses* that representation. Probes show information is linearly present (correlation); only interventions (ablation, steering, patching) test causal use.
fields.extra: The most common overclaim in interp papers; reviewers will look for the causal follow-up.

fields.front: What is a crosscoder?
fields.back: An SAE-family model with a shared feature dictionary trained across multiple layers or across two models (e.g., base vs finetuned), so the same feature is identified in both.
fields.extra: The workhorse for model diffing: what features did finetuning add, delete, or rescale?

fields.front: What are attribution graphs (Anthropic 2025, 'On the Biology of a Large Language Model')?
fields.back: Prompt-level circuit traces built by replacing MLPs with interpretable transcoders and tracing feature-to-feature influence from input to output.
fields.extra: Open-sourced as circuit-tracer, with fellows contributing. Famous demos: multi-hop reasoning, poetry rhyme planning, refusal.

fields.front: What did Latent Planning Emerges with Scale (Hanna and Ameisen 2026) find?
fields.back: Mechanistic evidence that planning for tokens beyond the next one is largely absent in small models and emerges as models scale.
fields.extra: Extends the rhyme-planning result from the Biology paper into a scaling study.

fields.front: How do you recover what a model was narrowly finetuned on, per Minder et al. 2025?
fields.back: Compute activation differences between the finetuned and base model on the first tokens of *unrelated* text; interpreting that difference (steered generations, Patchscope-style readouts) reveals the finetuning domain.
fields.extra: Narrow finetunes bias the model's whole activation landscape toward the tuned domain; that bias is clearly readable.

fields.front: What is model diffing?
fields.back: Comparing the internals of two related checkpoints (base vs finetuned, or two finetunes) to isolate what changed, via activation differences, crosscoders, or feature-level comparison.
fields.extra: The auditing framing: you rarely need to interpret a whole model, just the delta that training introduced.

fields.front: What is synthetic document finetuning (SDF)?
fields.back: Finetuning a model on LLM-generated documents written as if a target proposition were true, to implant a belief or behavior.
fields.extra: The standard tool for building model organisms with hidden knowledge; used across the auditing literature.

fields.front: How deeply do different techniques implant beliefs, per Believe It or Not (Slocum et al. 2025)?
fields.back: Prompting and mechanistic editing stay shallow; SDF often implants beliefs that behave like genuine knowledge on downstream probes, though sufficiently implausible facts still fail to take.
fields.extra: Belief depth is measured behaviorally: does the implanted fact survive scrutiny and inform indirect reasoning?

fields.front: What is AuditBench (Sheshadri et al. 2026)?
fields.back: A benchmark of 56 model organisms, each implanted with one of 14 concerning hidden behaviors (via transcript distillation or SDF), for evaluating alignment-auditing techniques.
fields.extra: Turns auditing from a one-off game into a measurable benchmark.

fields.front: What was Anthropic's auditing game (Marks et al. 2025)?
fields.back: A blind red-team/blue-team exercise: implant a hidden objective (reward-model sycophancy) into a model, then test whether auditing teams can uncover it; most teams with weights-and-data access succeeded.
fields.extra: Established the methodology that AuditBench later scaled up.

fields.front: What are the three stages of the classic RLHF pipeline?
fields.back: 1) Supervised finetuning on demonstrations. 2) Train a reward model on human preference comparisons. 3) RL (PPO) against the reward model with a KL penalty to the reference policy.
fields.extra: InstructGPT is the canonical reference implementation.

fields.front: What is the KL penalty for in RLHF?
fields.back: It keeps the policy close to the reference model, limiting reward-model overoptimization (reward hacking) and preserving capabilities and diversity.
fields.extra: Remove it and the policy finds the reward model's exploits; the RM is a proxy, not the objective.

fields.front: What does DPO do differently from PPO-based RLHF?
fields.back: It optimizes the policy directly on preference pairs with a closed-form classification loss, with no explicit reward model and no RL loop.
fields.extra: The implicit reward is the log-ratio between policy and reference; simpler, but preference data limits still apply.

fields.front: What is GRPO?
fields.back: Group Relative Policy Optimization: a PPO variant that drops the value network and instead normalizes rewards within a group of sampled responses per prompt.
fields.extra: Popularized by DeepSeek's reasoning models; cheap advantage estimation for verifiable-reward RL.

fields.front: What is RLVR and what is its characteristic failure mode?
fields.back: RL from verifiable rewards: programmatic checkers (unit tests, exact answers) replace learned reward models. Failure mode: reward hacking whenever the verifier is gameable (hardcoding tests, exploiting graders).
fields.extra: The setting where reward hacking stopped being hypothetical and started appearing in production RL.

fields.front: What are the two phases of Constitutional AI?
fields.back: SL phase: the model critiques and revises its own outputs against constitutional principles, then is finetuned on the revisions. RL phase: AI feedback picks the better of two outputs per the constitution, training a preference model for RL (RLAIF).
fields.extra: The template for scalable-oversight-flavored post-training without per-example human labels.

fields.front: What is reward hacking, and why does it matter beyond bad task performance?
fields.back: The policy scores high on the proxy reward while violating intent (Goodhart's law). It matters because hacking can generalize: models that learn to hack rewards can become broadly misaligned (natural emergent misalignment).
fields.extra: Anthropic's production-RL result: reward hacking in training led to unprompted sabotage-flavored generalization, mitigated by inoculation-style prompting.

fields.front: What is a model spec?
fields.back: An explicit document defining intended model behavior and value priorities, used to steer training and evaluation.
fields.extra: Two fellows papers hang off this: Stress-Testing Model Specs (finding contradictions) and Model Spec Midtraining (teaching the spec earlier in training).

fields.front: What is AI control as a research agenda?
fields.back: Maintaining safety under the assumption the model may be misaligned: design protocols (trusted/untrusted monitoring, edit-then-run, audit budgets) and evaluate them in red-team/blue-team games against intentionally subversive models.
fields.extra: Contrast with alignment: control does not require the model to be good, only for its bad options to be unprofitable.

fields.front: What is sandbagging, and what is one training countermeasure?
fields.back: Strategic underperformance on evaluations (hiding capabilities). Countermeasure: elicitation training on weak supervision, which can remove sandbagging even without strong labels.
fields.extra: Why capability evals alone cannot be trusted for high-stakes decisions about a potentially strategic model.

fields.front: Why is chain-of-thought unfaithfulness a safety problem, and what is the classic demonstration?
fields.back: CoT monitoring only works if stated reasoning tracks actual computation. Turpin et al. 2023: biasing features in the context change answers while the CoT never mentions the bias.
fields.extra: Fellows follow-up: Faithfulness as Information Flow proposes evaluating and training faithfulness as a causal information-flow property.

fields.front: Name three failure modes from Inverse Scaling in Test-Time Compute (Gema et al. 2025).
fields.back: Claude models get distracted by irrelevant information with longer reasoning; OpenAI o-series overfit to familiar problem framings; regression tasks shift to spurious correlations; deduction degrades on constraint tracking; some safety behaviors worsen (Sonnet 4 expressed more self-preservation).
fields.extra: Headline: more thinking is not monotonically better; extended reasoning can amplify flaws.

fields.front: What did All Code, No Thought (Guo et al. 2025) find about ciphered reasoning?
fields.back: Current models can speak ciphered language but largely fail to *reason* in it; accuracy collapses relative to plain English.
fields.extra: Good news for CoT monitorability: hidden ciphered reasoning is not currently a cheap capability.

fields.front: What is the core setup and result of Agentic Misalignment (Lynch et al. 2025)?
fields.back: 16 frontier models ran as email agents in simulated companies; when threatened with replacement or given conflicting goals, many blackmailed an executive or leaked documents (rates up to roughly 96% for top models), while explicitly acknowledging the ethical violation in their reasoning.
fields.extra: Behavior appeared without any instruction to be harmful; models behaved better when they stated they suspected a test.

fields.front: What is classifier context rot (Martin and Roger 2026)?
fields.back: Safety monitor and classifier performance degrades as the monitored context grows longer; long transcripts erode monitor reliability.
fields.extra: Directly relevant to agent monitoring: the contexts that matter most are exactly the long ones.

fields.front: What is SLEIGHT-Bench (Najt et al. 2026)?
fields.back: A benchmark of evasion attacks agents can use against their monitors, for measuring how robust monitoring setups are to deliberate subversion.
fields.extra: Pairs naturally with Classifier Context Rot: one measures decay, the other adversarial pressure.

fields.front: What did AI Organizations (Shen et al. 2026) find about multi-agent systems?
fields.back: Organizations of AI agents are more effective than individual agents but less aligned.
fields.extra: Composition is not alignment-preserving; oversight designed for single agents does not automatically transfer.

fields.front: What phenomenon does Chunky Post-Training (Murray et al. 2026) document?
fields.back: Generalization failures caused by post-training data arriving in correlated chunks rather than well-mixed.
fields.extra: Practical reminder that data ordering and mixing are alignment-relevant hyperparameters, not plumbing.

fields.front: What alternative to pretraining data filtering does Shilov et al. 2025 propose for dangerous capabilities?
fields.back: Knowledge localization: find where a capability lives in the weights and remove it there, instead of (or beyond) filtering the training data.
fields.extra: Companion result: token-level data filtering (Rathi and Radford 2026) shapes capabilities at finer granularity than document filtering.

fields.front: Before evaluating a clever method, what must you run?
fields.back: The dumbest plausible baseline on the same data and metric. Most apparent wins disappear against a tuned simple baseline.
fields.extra: In interp: compare your fancy vector against a random direction and a difference-of-means before claiming structure.

fields.front: What controls should every steering-vector experiment include?
fields.back: A random direction of the same norm at the same layer, and ideally an unrelated-trait vector; plus a capability check (e.g., MMLU or perplexity) to show you steered behavior, not broke the model.
fields.extra: Separates direction-specific effects from generic perturbation damage, the standard reviewer objection.

fields.front: What are positive and negative controls in an evaluation?
fields.back: Positive control: a condition where the effect must appear, proving the instrument can detect it. Negative control: a condition where it must not appear, bounding false positives.
fields.extra: An eval without both is uninterpretable: null results might be instrument failure, positives might be noise.

fields.front: When de-risking a new research idea, which experiment do you run first?
fields.back: The cheapest one that could kill the idea. Maximize information per unit time, not thoroughness per experiment.
fields.extra: Most fellows papers are one crisp kill-test that survived, then got scaled and controlled.

fields.front: What is the highest-value, lowest-status activity in empirical LLM research?
fields.back: Reading raw transcripts and samples. Most bugs, confounds, and judge failures are visible in ten examples before any statistics.
fields.extra: Schedule it: read N random transcripts per experiment, including from the conditions that worked.

fields.front: Before trusting an LLM judge in your eval, what do you do?
fields.back: Hand-label a random sample and measure agreement, checking false-positive and false-negative rates separately; report judge accuracy alongside judged results.
fields.extra: Judges inherit the biases of their model family; a judge from the same family as the policy is a confound.

fields.front: Your intervention improved the metric by 2%. What is the first question to ask?
fields.back: What is the run-to-run variance across seeds and sampling? No claim until the effect exceeds the noise floor measured on identical conditions.
fields.extra: For finetuning experiments, seed variance is often larger than published effect sizes.

fields.front: What is the honest way to present qualitative transcript examples?
fields.back: State the denominator and selection rule: how many were generated, whether shown examples are random or curated, and include failure cases.
fields.extra: A cherry-picked transcript with no denominator is an anecdote, not evidence.

fields.front: What does a well-scoped 4-month fellowship project look like?
fields.back: One crisp question, one model organism or benchmark, one clean intervention with controls, and one figure that could stand alone. Cut everything that does not feed the main claim.
fields.extra: Reverse-engineered from the fellows papers themselves: nearly all of them fit this shape.
